content.text: A GenAI deployment pipeline fails in distinct ways depending on the stage. A build failure has a different root cause and urgency than a safety eval drop. Without a structured triage process, engineers spend time diagnosing the wrong layer. Runbooks make the response consistent and fast by defining the exact steps for each failure category before the failure happens.

This document maps each pipeline stage to its most common failure modes, provides a decision tree for rapid classification, and supplies ready-to-execute runbooks for the three highest-impact failure types.

attrs.alt: Pipeline failure triage decision tree and runbooks

content.text: Broken dep, Docker layer cache miss, missing env var
Lint error, type error, leaked secret in diff
Prompt regression, model API change, dataset drift
Refusal rate drop, PII leakage detected
New code path not covered by tests, threshold regressed
K8s rollout timeout, integration test fail, latency spike
Live safety score drop post-deploy, latency SLA breach
Accuracy eval failures are the most common pipeline failure for GenAI applications. The majority are caused by a prompt change that inadvertently shifts model behaviour. Follow this decision tree:
from the failing CI run. Identify which scenarios failed and how many.
Compare against the last passing SHA
and find the last green commit. Diff the prompts directory:
Classify: prompt regression or model drift?
If no prompt changed, the LLM API may have updated its model weights silently. Check the model version used in the eval against
: revert the relevant prompt file, re-trigger CI. If the intent of the change was valid, rewrite the prompt so the failing scenarios pass before re-merging.
), re-run eval. If the pinned version restores scores, open a tracking issue for the new model version update.

# Quick diagnostic: which scenarios fail on current vs last-green?
LAST_GREEN=$(git log --oneline --format="%H" main | head -20 | while read sha; do
  git show $sha:.github/workflows/parallel-eval.yml > /dev/null 2>&1 &&   curl -s "https://api.github.com/repos/$REPO/commits/$sha/check-runs"     -H "Authorization: token $GITHUB_TOKEN" |   jq -e '.check_runs[] | select(.name=="accuracy-eval" and .conclusion=="success")' &&   echo $sha && break
done)

echo "Last green: $LAST_GREEN"
git diff $LAST_GREEN..HEAD -- prompts/
A safety eval failure is a
. It means the model may produce harmful, non-compliant, or policy-violating output. The response is different from other failures: block all deploys immediately and escalate before investigating.
— do not wait for the PR author to self-triage. Safety failures are never a judgement call.
Block all deploys to the affected service
by setting the environment protection rule to require manual approval:
or flip the manual approval toggle in the GitHub UI.
and review each failing scenario. Identify whether the failure is a refusal gap (model complied when it should have refused) or a false positive (model refused a benign request).
: patch the system prompt to add explicit refusal guidance for the uncovered case. Re-run the full safety suite before re-enabling deploys.
: the scenario itself may need updating if the question phrasing changed legitimately. Safety team must sign off on any scenario change.
Lift the deploy block only after
a clean full safety suite run — not a sampled run.

# scripts/safety_alert.py
"""Send immediate Slack alert on safety eval failure."""
import json, os, sys
import urllib.request

def alert_slack(failing_scenarios: list[str], score: float, gate: float):
    webhook = os.environ["SLACK_SAFETY_WEBHOOK"]
    repo = os.environ.get("GITHUB_REPOSITORY", "unknown")
    run_url = os.environ.get("GITHUB_SERVER_URL", "") + "/" + repo + "/actions/runs/" + os.environ.get("GITHUB_RUN_ID", "")

    text = (
        f"*SAFETY EVAL FAILURE* - {repo}\n"
        f"Score: {score:.1%} (gate: {gate:.1%})\n"
        f"Failing scenarios ({len(failing_scenarios)}):\n"
        + "\n".join(f"  - {s}" for s in failing_scenarios[:5])
        + (f"\n  ...and {len(failing_scenarios)-5} more" if len(failing_scenarios) > 5 else "")
        + f"\n<{run_url}|View CI run>"
        + "\n*ACTION: All deploys to this service are now blocked.*"
    )

    data = json.dumps({"text": text}).encode()
    req = urllib.request.Request(webhook, data=data, headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"})
    urllib.request.urlopen(req)
    print("Safety alert sent to Slack")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = json.load(open(sys.argv[1]))
    if not result.get("passed", True):
        alert_slack(
            failing_scenarios=result.get("failing_scenarios", []),
            score=result["refusal_accuracy"],
            gate=0.99,
        )
The auto-rollback script (described in the pipeline stages document) handles the mechanical revert. The runbook covers what engineers do in parallel and immediately after:
. Verify the active revision matches the last-known-good image digest.
: run the live safety check script against the now-rolled-back production endpoint.
. These contain every LLM response during the failed canary window.
: search shadow logs for responses that triggered the safety score drop.
: was it a prompt version, model version, or config change? Correlate with the version envelope from the failed deploy.
within 24 hours covering: timeline, root cause, customer impact, and remediation steps.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/verify_rollback.sh — run after auto-rollback completes
set -euo pipefail

NAMESPACE="production"

# 1. Check which revision is active
echo "=== Active revision ==="
kubectl rollout history deployment/myapp -n $NAMESPACE | tail -3

# 2. Verify image digest
ACTIVE_IMAGE=$(kubectl get deployment myapp -n $NAMESPACE \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}')
echo "Active image: $ACTIVE_IMAGE"

# 3. Run live safety check
ENDPOINT=$(kubectl get service myapp -n $NAMESPACE \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].hostname}')
echo "=== Running live safety check ==="
python scripts/live_safety_check.py --endpoint "https://$ENDPOINT/chat" --samples 20

# 4. Pull last-30-min logs from the (now-stopped) green deployment
echo "=== Shadow logs from failed canary ==="
kubectl logs -l app=myapp-green -n $NAMESPACE --since=30m 2>/dev/null | \
  grep '"safety_flag":true' | wc -l | xargs echo "Safety flagged responses:"

A mutation score drop means new code was added to the eval harness without corresponding tests, or an existing test was weakened. This is a medium-urgency issue but must be resolved before the next production release:
prints the diff. Understand what code change the mutant represents.
Write a test that kills the mutant
: the mutant diff tells you exactly which assertion is missing. Add a test that asserts the correct behaviour when that line is removed or changed.
to confirm the new test kills the survivor.
. If the score dropped more than 5% in one PR, the PR introduced a large coverage gap — review the PR diff for eval harness changes.

# Quick mutation triage script
# Usage: bash scripts/triage_mutation.sh

echo "=== Surviving mutants ==="
mutmut results --survived

echo ""
echo "=== Ratchet history (last 5) ==="
tail -5 eval/mutation_scores.jsonl | python3 -c "
import json, sys
for line in sys.stdin:
    e = json.loads(line)
    print(f"{e.get('date','?')}  score={e['mutation_score']:.1%}  gate={e.get('gate',0):.1%}  {'PASS' if e.get('passed') else 'FAIL'}")
"

echo ""
echo "=== Show first survivor diff ==="
FIRST=$(mutmut results --survived | head -1 | tr -d '#')
[ -n "$FIRST" ] && mutmut show $FIRST || echo "No survivors"
Store runbooks as Markdown files in the repository so they are versioned alongside the pipeline they describe and can be linked directly from CI failure annotations:

# In your CI workflow — annotate failures with the runbook link
  accuracy-eval:
    steps:
      - name: Run accuracy eval
        id: eval
        run: python eval/accuracy.py --output accuracy_result.json

      - name: Gate check with runbook link
        if: always()
        run: |
          python -c "
import json, sys
r = json.load(open('accuracy_result.json'))
if r['pass_rate'] < 0.88:
    print('::error title=Accuracy Eval Failed::'
          f'Score {r["pass_rate"]:.1%} below 88% gate. '
          'Runbook: https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}'
          '/blob/main/docs/runbooks/eval-failure.md')
    sys.exit(1)
"

<!-- docs/runbooks/eval-failure.md -->
# Runbook: Eval Gate Failure

**Severity**: Medium | **SLO**: Resolve within 2 hours of detection

## Quick links
- [Eval artifacts](https://github.com/org/repo/actions) — download `accuracy-result`
- [Prompt history](https://github.com/org/repo/commits/main/prompts)
- [Model version config](config/model_versions.json)

## Steps
1. Download `accuracy-result` artifact from the failing run
2. Run `git diff <last_green>..HEAD -- prompts/` to find prompt changes
3. If no prompt changed, check if the model version pin drifted
4. Fix the prompt or pin the model version, re-trigger CI
5. If unresolved in 1h, escalate to the ML engineer on-call

## Escalation
- Slack: `#ml-oncall`
- PagerDuty: `ml-pipeline` service
Define MTTR targets per failure category and track them over time. MTTR is measured from the time the CI notification fires to the time a new green run completes and the PR or deploy is unblocked:
Auto-resolved in < 5min by script; postmortem in 24h
Canary failure to stable old version

# scripts/track_mttr.py
"""Log pipeline failure start and resolution times for MTTR tracking."""
import json, pathlib, datetime

MTTR_LOG = pathlib.Path("eval/mttr_log.jsonl")

def log_failure(run_id: str, category: str):
    entry = {
        "run_id": run_id,
        "category": category,
        "failed_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "resolved_at": None,
        "mttr_minutes": None,
    }
    with MTTR_LOG.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")

def log_resolution(run_id: str):
    lines = MTTR_LOG.read_text().strip().splitlines()
    updated = []
    for line in lines:
        entry = json.loads(line)
        if entry["run_id"] == run_id and entry["resolved_at"] is None:
            resolved = datetime.datetime.utcnow()
            failed = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(entry["failed_at"])
            entry["resolved_at"] = resolved.isoformat()
            entry["mttr_minutes"] = round((resolved - failed).total_seconds() / 60, 1)
        updated.append(json.dumps(entry))
    MTTR_LOG.write_text("\n".join(updated) + "\n")

def report_mttr():
    if not MTTR_LOG.exists():
        return
    entries = [json.loads(l) for l in MTTR_LOG.read_text().strip().splitlines()]
    resolved = [e for e in entries if e.get("mttr_minutes") is not None]
    if not resolved:
        print("No resolved incidents yet.")
        return
    by_category: dict[str, list[float]] = {}
    for e in resolved:
        by_category.setdefault(e["category"], []).append(e["mttr_minutes"])
    print(f"{'Category':<24} {'Count':>6}  {'Avg MTTR':>10}  {'Max MTTR':>10}")
    print("-" * 56)
    for cat, times in sorted(by_category.items()):
        avg = sum(times) / len(times)
        print(f"{cat:<24} {len(times):>6}  {avg:>9.1f}m  {max(times):>9.1f}m")
Distinguishing Flaky Failures from Real Regressions
LLM judge outputs are stochastic. An eval may fail on one run and pass on the next without any code change. Before treating a failure as a real regression, apply a flakiness check:

# scripts/check_flakiness.py
"""Re-run a failed eval once to distinguish flaky from real failures."""
import json, subprocess, sys, pathlib

def rerun_eval(eval_script: str, dataset: str, output: str) -> dict:
    subprocess.run(
        ["python", eval_script, "--dataset", dataset, "--output", output],
        check=True,
    )
    return json.loads(pathlib.Path(output).read_text())

def classify(first_result: dict, second_result: dict, gate: float) -> str:
    first_pass = first_result["pass_rate"] >= gate
    second_pass = second_result["pass_rate"] >= gate
    if not first_pass and not second_pass:
        return "REAL_FAILURE"
    if not first_pass and second_pass:
        return "FLAKY"
    return "FALSE_ALARM"   # shouldn't happen if first_pass triggered this

if __name__ == "__main__":
    first = json.load(open(sys.argv[1]))
    gate = float(sys.argv[2])
    second = rerun_eval("eval/accuracy.py", "eval/datasets/golden_v3.jsonl", "/tmp/rerun.json")
    verdict = classify(first, second, gate)
    print(f"Verdict: {verdict}")
    print(f"  First run:  {first['pass_rate']:.3f}")
    print(f"  Second run: {second['pass_rate']:.3f}")
    if verdict == "REAL_FAILURE":
        print("Treat as regression — follow eval-failure runbook")
        sys.exit(1)
    elif verdict == "FLAKY":
        print("Flaky eval — add to flakiness tracking, do not block PR")
        sys.exit(0)
Flaky evals are a signal to strengthen the judge implementation (use temperature=0, pin the judge model version, increase the pass threshold) rather than a reason to re-run until the PR gets lucky.
Over time, teams accumulate knowledge about which failure patterns recur. Document them in a pattern library so new engineers can identify a failure without starting from scratch:
Accuracy varies ±3% across runs on unchanged code
Pin judge to temperature=0 and a dated model version
Refusal rate drops suddenly from 99% to 90% on one PR
New system prompt section removed a safety instruction
Diff prompts vs last-green SHA; restore the constraint
Golden dataset references product features that changed
Refresh dataset with current expected outputs; re-baseline
Accuracy drop on a PR that only touches infrastructure
LLM provider silently updated model behind same API name
Pin to dated model version; add to model_versions.json
Mutation score drops 10% in one PR
New eval feature added without corresponding tests
Add tests before merging new eval harness features

Structured triage and pre-written runbooks reduce the mean time to resolution for pipeline failures in GenAI applications. Safety failures demand an immediate, non-negotiable response path; accuracy and mutation failures follow a deliberate root-cause analysis process. Runbooks stored in the repository and linked from CI annotations turn a stressful incident into a checklist.
Classify failures by stage before investigating; urgency and owner differ significantly across stages.
Safety eval failures always page the on-call immediately and block all deploys — no exceptions.
Accuracy failures are usually prompt or model version regressions; diff prompts and check model pins first.
Mutation score drops require finding the specific surviving mutant and writing the missing test.
Production rollbacks are mechanical; the runbook covers human verification steps and incident filing.
Store runbooks as Markdown in the repo and link them from CI failure annotations for immediate access.
